title: Benefits of Grape for Vision. Building upon the understanding of how dietary antioxidants such as vitamin A, lutein, zeaxanthin, and polyphenols contribute to ocular health, it is valuable to explore specific foods rich in these nutrients. Grapes, in particular, have garnered attention due to their potential benefits for eye health. Grapes are abundant in a variety of bioactive compounds, including stilbenes like resveratrol and piceatannol, flavonoids such as catechins and quercetin, and small amounts of carotenoids like lutein and zeaxanthin. These nutrients and bioactive compounds possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective properties that may contribute to the prevention or slowing of ocular diseases like A M D, cataracts, and diabetic retinopathy.

sentences.text: Benefits of Grape for Vision.

Building upon the understanding of how dietary antioxidants such as vitamin A, lutein, zeaxanthin, and polyphenols contribute to ocular health, it is valuable to explore specific foods rich in these nutrients.
Grapes, in particular, have garnered attention due to their potential benefits for eye health.

Grapes are abundant in a variety of bioactive compounds, including stilbenes like resveratrol and piceatannol, flavonoids such as catechins and quercetin, and small amounts of carotenoids like lutein and zeaxanthin.

These nutrients and bioactive compounds possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective properties that may contribute to the prevention or slowing of ocular diseases like A M D, cataracts, and diabetic retinopathy.
The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of grapes have also been studied in relation to multiple ocular diseases.

In vitro studies using human R P E cells demonstrated that grape polyphenols protected against chemically induced endoplasmic reticulum stress and suppressed pro-inflammatory gene transcription in response to tumor necrosis factor-alpha.

Building on these findings, the group later administered grape polyphenol extracts to mice at 100 to 200 milligrams per kilogram body weight for 7 days prior to inducing ocular inflammation via the intravitreal injection of lipopolysaccharide.
Pretreatment with grape polyphenols significantly reduced ocular inflammation, decreased pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, reduced leukocyte infiltration, and minimized retinal tissue damage.
Grape seed extract, rich in proanthocyanidins, has also been investigated for its antioxidant effects.
Furthermore, Wan and colleagues reported significant reductions in R P E cellular senescence following grape seed extract supplementation in in vitro A M D models.
Animal studies further underscore the potential benefits of grape consumption for maintaining and improving overall eye health.

Research has explored the effects of grape consumption on retinal aging and the progression of A M D, demonstrating protective effects against oxidative stress and inflammation that contribute to these conditions.
A previous animal study induced retinal degeneration in mice through acute oxidative stress using subretinal injection of paraquat.
Compared to mice fed with the control diet, mice on the grape-supplemented diet showed maintenance of retinal thickness and photoreceptor numbers with oxidative stress.
Moreover, there was a preservation of retinal function after oxidative stress injury in mice on a grape diet compared with mice on a control diet.

Another in vivo study conducted by Sun and colleagues demonstrated that grape seed extract exerts a protective effect on the retina by ameliorating oxidative-stress-mediated injury through the activation of the Nrf2 pathway in Wistar rats.
In a study by Yu and colleagues, researchers used a mouse model deficient in the integrin receptor alpha-v-beta-5, which resulted in impaired phagocytosis by R P E cells.
This impairment results in the accumulation of photoreceptor outer segment debris, lipofuscin buildup, oxidative damage, and gradual vision loss, paralleling aspects of atrophic A M D in humans.

When these mice were supplemented with a grape-enriched diet, equivalent to a human consumption rate of approximately 3.5 servings of grapes per day throughout adulthood from 3 to 12 months of age, there was significant prevention of lipofuscin accumulation, a reduction in oxidative protein damage, and the preservation of visual function.
Interestingly, grape consumption did not correct the primary phagocytic defect but provided neuroprotective effects.
Early and continuous grape consumption was most effective; starting supplementation at older ages, 9 to 12 months, did not prevent vision loss.
This suggests that early dietary intervention with grapes may have protective effects against retinal aging and A M D progression.
Further research has examined grape supplementation in the context of neovascular A M D. Kanavi investigated the effects of grape consumption on choroidal neovascularization in wild-type mice.

The mice received drinking water supplemented with dissolved grape powder for 12 days prior to laser-induced injury, which is commonly used to simulate choroidal neovascularization in experimental models.
Grape supplementation significantly reduced the severity of abnormal blood vessel growth, showing efficacy comparable to resveratrol treatment.
Continuous low-dose consumption appeared more beneficial than single high-dose administration, implying that a consistent intake of grape phytonutrients enhances their protective effects.
In addition to its potential benefits for A M D and retinal health, grape consumption may also play a role in preventing cataract formation.
Liu and colleagues investigated the effects of a grape-enriched diet on ultraviolet-induced cataract development in mouse models.
The researchers found that mice fed a grape-supplemented diet exhibited a significant reduction in cataract formation following ultraviolet radiation exposure compared to control mice.
This protective effect was linked to the anti-inflammatory properties of grapes, mediated through the activation of the Nrf2 pathway.
This activation led to the upregulation of antioxidant enzymes and increased the expression of X-linked inhibitor of apoptosis protein, which inhibits apoptosis in lens epithelial cells.
These findings indicate that grape consumption may strengthen the eye's natural defense mechanisms against oxidative stress and apoptosis, thereby potentially reducing the risk of cataract formation.
While most research has been preclinical, emerging human studies suggest the potential benefits of grape consumption for eye health.

A pilot clinical study investigated the eye-beneficial effects of 6-month grape seed extract supplementation on A M D patients, revealing a significant reduction in central retinal thickness and a concomitant improvement in visual acuity.

Another 6-month randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trial involving 99 patients with mild-to-moderate nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy demonstrated that supplementation effectively reduced retinal swelling and oxidative stress.
A recent 16-week randomized controlled trial conducted in Singapore examined the effects of freeze-dried grape powder consumption on macular pigment accumulation in older adults.

Participants consuming 46 grams per day of freeze-dried grape powder showed a significant increase in macular pigment optical density from baseline 0.56 plus or minus 0.04 to 0.61 plus or minus 0.04 units, unlike the control group.
This enhancement was accompanied by increased plasma total antioxidant capacity and total phenolic content, suggesting improved systemic antioxidant status.

Moreover, the freeze-dried grape powder group exhibited stabilization of skin advanced glycation end products, baseline 2.38 plus or minus 0.15 to 2.42 plus or minus 0.11, whereas the control group showed a significant increase in skin advanced glycation end products over the study period, baseline 2.47 plus or minus 0.24 to 2.99 plus or minus 0.12.
Advanced glycation end products can accumulate in eye tissues, inducing oxidative stress and damaging the vascular components of the retina, thereby contributing to the development of eye diseases.

The stabilization of skin advanced glycation end products in the freeze-dried grape powder group suggests that grape polyphenols may modulate advanced glycation end product accumulation, potentially extending this regulatory effect to ocular tissues and supporting overall eye health.
This highlights the potential benefits of grape consumption for maintaining and enhancing eye health in aging populations.
This collective evidence underscores the potential of grapes and grape-derived compounds in promoting eye health.

The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of grapes may help prevent or slow the progression of retinal diseases by reducing oxidative damage, inhibiting abnormal blood vessel growth, and modulating inflammatory responses.
Despite these promising indications, current evidence from human clinical studies is limited, and more research is necessary to establish a definitive link between grape consumption and eye health.
Factors such as bioavailability, optimal dosage, and long-term effects need to be thoroughly investigated.
